category_title: Low-achieving students who disengage or feel devalued during group work
minimal_context: The student is working in a mixed-ability group to agree on answers for a multiple-choice test during the second part of the lesson.
dialogue:
TUTOR: How is your group doing with the multiple-choice test? Remember to make your decisions together.

STUDENT: The better students are going too fast. I got stuck trying to explain my idea and they just jumped into my mouth.

TUTOR: Make sure you are participating and sharing your thoughts for the next questions.

STUDENT: I feel uncomfortable. My opinions aren't respected and the group decisions are mostly made by only one or two members.

TUTOR: If they are going too fast, you should ask them to explain the information to you.

STUDENT: (Exhibits off-task behavior by turning away from the test) I'm not going to risk asking for additional explanations. I'm in a jobless situation with nothing to do but listen passively, so I'm just going to fall to sleep.

options.A: Tell the student, "Don't say you can't, you can," and prompt them to continue.
options.B: Provide an open, discovery-based activity where there are no single correct answers.
options.C: Give the student a warning about falling asleep but do not discipline them further.
options.D: Continue with the multiple-choice test rather than interrupt the group's flow by disciplining.
